name: Divisibility Rules — Quick Checks
whatIsIt: Divisibility rules let you tell if one number divides another EXACTLY, without doing the full division. By 2: even last digit. By 5: ends in 0 or 5. By 10: ends in 0. By 3: digit sum divisible by 3. By 9: digit sum divisible by 9. By 4: last two digits divisible by 4. By 6: divisible by 2 AND 3.
whyNeeded: These quick checks make factoring, simplifying fractions and mental maths fast. Instead of long division you glance at the number and know — a real time-saver for the rest of maths.
realLifeUses: Sharing 126 sweets equally among 3 or 6 friends — will it be even?
Simplifying a fraction by spotting a common factor
Checking if a number of items packs neatly into boxes of 4
Splitting a bill evenly among people

vocabulary.meaning: The sum of all the digits (for 342 it is 3+4+2 = 9).
A number ending in 0, 2, 4, 6 or 8 (divisible by 2).

story.title: The Prize Packets
story.text: Sir has 342 stickers and asks Dev if they'll share equally among 3 groups. Dev doesn't divide — he adds the digits: 3 + 4 + 2 = 9, and 9 is divisible by 3. 'Yes Sir, 342 divides by 3!' Sir is impressed: 'You used the rule instead of long division.'
story.extractedProblem: Is 342 divisible by 3? Use the digit-sum rule.

visual.caption: Multiples of 3 all have a digit sum divisible by 3. Rules spot them without dividing.

teachingGallery.title: The quick rules
teachingGallery.note: Look at last digit(s) or the digit sum.

examples.caption: Digit sum divisible by 9 → divisible by 9.

standardMethod.summary: Match the number to the rule for that divisor — check the last digit(s) or the digit sum.
steps: By 2, 5, 10: look at the LAST digit (even; 0 or 5; 0).
By 3 or 9: add the digits; if the sum divides by 3 (or 9), so does the number.
By 4: check if the last TWO digits divide by 4.
By 6: it must pass BOTH the 2-rule and the 3-rule.
So 435 is divisible by both 5 and 3.
By 4: look at the last two digits, 28.

commonMistakes.mistake: Using the last digit for the 3-rule (thinking 13 divides by 3 because it 'looks' like it).
commonMistakes.fix: For 3 and 9 use the DIGIT SUM, not the last digit. 1 + 3 = 4, so 13 is NOT divisible by 3.

commonMistakes.mistake: Saying a number divides by 6 after checking only the 2-rule (or only the 3-rule).
commonMistakes.fix: By 6 you need BOTH: divisible by 2 AND by 3. Check both.

options.label: Yes — it ends in an even digit

easy.answer: Yes — it ends in an even digit

options.label: Yes — even AND digit sum 6 divides by 3

challenge.answer: Yes — even AND digit sum 6 divides by 3

challenge.q: Ravi says 13 divides by 3 'because it ends in 3'. What's the fix?

options.label: Use the digit sum: 1+3 = 4, not divisible by 3

challenge.answer: Use the digit sum: 1+3 = 4, not divisible by 3

options.label: No — it ends in an odd digit

questions.answer: No — it ends in an odd digit

questions.q: For divisibility by 6 you must check…

teachBackPrompt: Give a grown-up a 3-digit number and quickly say which of 2, 3, 5, 9 and 10 divide it — then prove one with the actual division. Explain the digit-sum trick for 3 and 9.

revisionCard.summary: ÷2: even last digit. ÷5: ends 0 or 5. ÷10: ends 0. ÷3: digit sum ÷3. ÷9: digit sum ÷9. ÷4: last two digits ÷4. ÷6: passes BOTH 2 and 3. Use digit SUM for 3 and 9 (not the last digit).

rememberIt.unpack: 4: last two digits /4. 6: divisible by 2 AND 3. 8: last three digits /8.

studentQuestions.a: Add the digits. If the total is divisible by 3, so is the number. 123 -> 1+2+3 = 6 -> yes.

studentQuestions.q: How do I check divisible by 6?
studentQuestions.a: It must pass BOTH the 2-rule (even) and the 3-rule (digit sum divisible by 3).
